title: Friendship & Cooperation
objective: Students will explore friendship & cooperation concepts including Resolving Disagreements with Friends, Friendships change over time, Working Well in a Group and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Resolving Disagreements with Friends? Let's find out together!
Resolve simple disagreements with peers by talking it through — explaining how they feel, listening to the other person's side, and finding a compromise or solution that both can accept
Let me show you something cool. Explain their own point of view calmly during a disagreement
If you and a friend disagree about which game to play at break time, can they talk it through and find a compromise — rather than one person always getting their way or the argument escalating?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Resolving Disagreements with Friends. Explain their own point of view calmly during a disagreement Listen to the other person's perspective without interrupting
Let's explore Friendships change over time! Have you ever wondered about this?

Understand that friendships change over time — that it's normal for friends to drift apart or for new friendships to form — and develop strategies for making new friends and handling friendship changes without feeling like something is wrong with them
Time to try this out: Explain that friendships changing is normal and doesn't mean something is wrong
If you's best friend starts spending more time with someone else, can they handle it without falling apart — understanding that friendships shift and they can make new friends too?

Great job! Today we learned about Friendships change over time. Explain that friendships changing is normal and doesn't mean something is wrong Describe a strategy for making new friends, such as joining a club or inviting someone to play
Have you ever thought about Working Well in a Group? Let's find out together!
Work effectively as part of a small group — contributing their own ideas, listening to others' ideas, taking on a fair share of the work, and supporting the group to reach a shared goal
Time to try this out: Contribute at least one idea during a group discussion
When you has to do a group project at school, do they pull their weight — sharing ideas and doing their part — rather than sitting back or trying to take over?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Working Well in a Group. Contribute at least one idea during a group discussion Listen to and build on another group member's idea
Let's explore Communication Vocabulary! Have you ever wondered about this?

Know and use the vocabulary of healthy communication and conflict — assertive, passive, aggressive, compromise, conflict, resolution, mediate, bystander, upstander, and peer pressure — and understand the difference between these contrasting approaches
Let's practice together! Use 'assertive' correctly and distinguish it from 'aggressive' — e.g. 'Being assertive means saying what I need calmly'
If you and a friend have a disagreement, can they describe it using words like 'conflict' or 'compromise' — and explain how assertive communication is different from aggressive or passive behaviour?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Communication Vocabulary. Use 'assertive' correctly and distinguish it from 'aggressive' — e.g. 'Being assertive means saying what I need calmly' Explain what 'conflict resolution' means and describe one strategy such as using 'I feel...' statements
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Roles in a Group!
Understand different roles people play in groups — leader, supporter, mediator, idea-generator — and recognise that effective groups need a mix of roles, not everyone trying to be the leader
Let's practice together! Name at least three different roles people can play in a group
In a group project, can you recognise that they don't always have to be in charge — and that sometimes the group works better when they support someone else's idea rather than pushing their own?
Great job! Today we learned about Roles in a Group. Name at least three different roles people can play in a group Identify their own preferred role and describe its strengths
